data.title: South32 Resets Hermosa Taylor With Higher Costs And Longer Mine Life
data.description:
• South32 (ASX:S32) has flagged about US$1.1b in additional capital expenditure for the Taylor project at its Hermosa asset, taking total projected spend to roughly US$3.3b.
  • First production has been pushed back from mid 2027 to mid 2028, with full capacity now targeted for FY2031, one year later than previously indicated.
  • The update includes a 52% uplift in Ore Reserves, an extended mine life to around 33 years, and extra decline infrastructure aimed at improving long term operating flexibility.

For investors watching ASX:S32 at around A$4.12, this Hermosa Taylor update combines higher upfront costs and timing delays with a materially larger ore base and much longer potential mine life. The share price is up 16.1% year to date and 49.6% over the past year, even after a 4.6% decline over the past week and an 8.0% decline over the past month, so this project reset lands after a strong run in the stock.

From here, attention is likely to focus on how South32 manages execution risk at Taylor, including contractor performance and cost control, in the context of a longer life asset. For readers, the key questions are whether the enlarged resource, 33 year mine life and planned infrastructure changes provide enough long term value to justify the higher US$3.3b capital bill and the shift in the production timeline.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for South32 by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on South32.

📰 Beyond the headline: 1 risk and 3 things going right for South32 that every investor should see.

This update effectively reframes Hermosa Taylor as a larger, more complex long life growth project rather than a quick new source of cash flow. The US$1.1b uplift in growth capital to about US$3.3b, together with a one year delay to first production and to nameplate capacity, increases execution risk and ties up more capital for longer. At the same time, the 52% Ore Reserve uplift, 17% higher life of mine production and roughly 33 year mine life extend South32’s exposure to zinc, lead and silver, with Peake and Clark adding copper and flexibility options over time. For readers, the trade off is clear: a bigger multi decade ore system and integrated underground plan versus higher upfront spend, contractor performance issues and revised operating cost assumptions at roughly US$100 per tonne.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The South32 Narrative

  • The Hermosa update aligns with the existing focus on building future facing base metals exposure through Taylor, Sierra Gorda and related copper options, reinforcing South32’s shift away from coal and toward longer life zinc, lead, silver and potential copper production.
  • The increased capital budget, shaft construction challenges and later ramp up to FY2031 highlight exactly the project execution and cost inflation risks that the narrative already flags as potential pressure points for future cash flow and returns.
  • The specific integration of Clark decline access, extra ore handling capacity and Peake’s 32% larger copper resource, along with the updated US$3.1b to US$4.5b NPV range, may not be fully reflected in existing storyline assumptions about Hermosa’s optionality.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for South32 to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Higher growth capital of about US$3.3b, concentrated between Q4 FY2026 and H2 FY2028, raises funding and cost overrun risk if inflation, US tariffs or input prices move further or if additional scope changes emerge.
  • ⚠️ Contractor underperformance on shaft construction and the need for strengthened oversight underline execution risk, which could affect timing, unit costs and ultimately project returns if productivity does not stabilise.
  • 🎁 A 52% increase in Ore Reserves to 99 Mt, a roughly 33 year initial operating life and 17% higher life of mine production support Hermosa Taylor’s role as a long duration, low cost producer of zinc, lead and silver.
  • 🎁 The Peake copper resource uplift to 33 Mt and the Clark decline link offer additional sources of future copper production and mine life flexibility that could differentiate South32 versus diversified peers with US growth options such as BHP or Rio Tinto.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, the key watchpoints are whether South32 can improve shaft sinking productivity under the revised plan, hold operating unit costs near the US$100 per tonne assumption and deliver the more gradual ramp up to FY2031 without further delays. Investors may also want to track how much of the roughly US$2.1b remaining growth capital through H2 FY2028 is committed to contracted pricing, any further updates on Peake’s potential copper link into Taylor and whether additional surface infrastructure de bottlenecking actually supports production above current design rates.

data.title: Is Lithia Motors (LAD) Pricing Look Attractive After Recent Share Price Rebound
data.description:

  • The stock has recently seen a 5.8% move over 7 days and a 16.0% change over 30 days, while the return year to date is a 12.5% decline and the 1 year return is a 1.1% decline, with a 37.5% gain over 3 years and a 20.9% decline over 5 years.

These headlines provide background for the recent share price moves, especially as investors consider how the sector fits into their broader portfolios.

Find out why Lithia Motors's -1.1% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Lithia Motors Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those cash flows back to today using a required rate of return.

For Lithia Motors, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections in $. The latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of $422.6 million. Analyst and extrapolated estimates then move from an expected free cash outflow of $790.0 million in 2026 to free cash inflows that reach $1,549.0 million by 2030, with intermediate yearly projections in between. After 2030, Simply Wall St extrapolates further free cash flow estimates through 2035 as part of the second stage of the model.

When all these projected cash flows are discounted back and added up, the resulting estimated intrinsic value is about $579.05 per share, compared with a current share price of around $290.90. On this basis, the DCF output suggests Lithia Motors is about 49.8% undervalued.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Lithia Motors.

Approach 2: Lithia Motors Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company, the P/E ratio is a useful way to gauge what you are paying for each dollar of earnings and to compare that price with other businesses. Higher growth expectations or lower perceived risk often justify a higher “normal” P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually point to a lower one.

Lithia Motors currently trades on a P/E of 9.54x. That sits well below the Specialty Retail industry average P/E of 19.88x and the broader peer group average of 23.22x. On the surface, this suggests the market is assigning a lower earnings multiple to Lithia Motors than to many comparable companies.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Lithia Motors is 17.62x. This is a proprietary P/E estimate that reflects factors such as the company’s earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market capitalization and risk characteristics. Because it adjusts for these company specific inputs, the Fair Ratio can be more informative than a simple comparison with peers or the industry average.

Comparing the Fair Ratio of 17.62x with the current P/E of 9.54x indicates that Lithia Motors is trading below this modelled level.

Approach 1: Provident Financial Services Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a company is expected to generate over and above the return that investors require for holding its equity.
